introduction.body_text:
In Magic: the Gathering, White represents structure, order, and the pursuit of stability through rules, balance, and disciplined play. This deck excels at creating environments where efficient sequencing and small advantages accumulate over time.

With this deck, those principles are applied to recursion—specifically, returning low-cost creatures to the battlefield over and over again through historic triggers.

This deck wins by assembling pieces.

Rather than relying on a single powerful card, it is built around interactions where A + B + C produces a result greater than any individual part. A low-cost creature, a historic spell, and a sacrifice or loop outlet can combine into a self-sustaining engine. Once established, that engine converts every small action into material advantage—cards, mana, or board presence—until the game state collapses.

Because of this, your goal is not to “play big threats.” Your goal is to identify which pieces you are missing, assemble them efficiently, and protect the system long enough for it to take over the game.

This deck rewards sequencing, planning, and recognizing when your position has shifted from setup to conversion. When your pieces are in place, even the smallest spell can become the start of a winning line.

why_play.body_text:
You should play this deck if you enjoy building toward a win rather than racing to one.

This is a system-driven deck. It asks you to evaluate what matters, identify missing pieces, and assemble them in the correct order. Every game becomes a puzzle: what do you have, what do you need, and how do you get there before the table can stop you?

This deck is also an excellent entry point for players learning Commander. It keeps mana requirements straightforward while introducing deeper concepts like sequencing, recursion, and resource conversion. You can focus on how your cards interact and build advantage without being overwhelmed by complex mana bases.

If you enjoy turning small interactions into decisive outcomes—and learning how to recognize when a game is yours to win—this deck delivers that experience.

posture.body_text:
This deck operates under a Combo posture within the exploit triangle. It is not trying to win through early pressure or long-term suppression. It is trying to assemble a functional state and convert it into a win.

Against Aggro (Compression) decks, you are advantaged once your engine is online. Their plan is to reduce your resources quickly, but your ability to generate value from small pieces allows you to stabilize and then outscale them. In these matchups, your role is to survive long enough to assemble.

Against Control (Suppression) decks, you are being exploited. They are trying to prevent your pieces from functioning together. Here, you must act as the “beats” player—identify that you cannot wait indefinitely, and commit to assembling your engine before they can fully lock the game down. Hesitation favors them.

Understanding who has the initiative is critical. If the table allows you time, you can build toward inevitability. If your opponents are positioned to disrupt you, you must recognize that and act first to change the status quo.

Your success with this deck comes from correctly identifying your role in each game:
- assemble when you have time
- accelerate when you do not
- convert immediately when your system is live

This is not a deck that wins by accident. It wins when you recognize that you are ready—and take the game.

introduction.body_text:
In Magic: the Gathering, White represents structure, order, and the pursuit of stability through rules, balance, and disciplined play.

With Celestial Kirin at the center, The Spiritual Path applies those ideas through a control posture built on patience, interaction, and making the game proceed on your terms instead of the table's.

This deck wins by making the game proceed on your terms.

Rather than relying on a narrow A + B + C assembly or the repeated pressure of A, A, A, A redundancy, control derives advantage over time through superior card quality, patient interaction, and the ability to make opposing plans function worse. Its best games are not rushed. They are shaped.

Because of this, your goal is to trade efficiently, suppress the lines that matter, and let the table's best options get worse while yours improve. Going for it with control usually means recognizing when you no longer need to ask if the game can be stabilized and can start asking how that stability converts into victory.

This deck rewards patience, restraint, and the discipline to interact where it changes the status quo instead of where it merely feels active.

why_play.body_text:
You should play this deck if you enjoy patience, interaction, and winning after making the table operate inside a structure you imposed.

This is a decision-heavy deck. It asks you to recognize what actually matters, spend interaction where it changes the game, and derive advantage over time through better timing and superior card quality. Every game becomes a question of how long you can keep the table off balance before turning stability into inevitability.

These decks are great choices for players learning Commander because staying in one color lets you focus on sequencing, threat assessment, and card interaction without also managing a complicated mana base or color fixing.

If you want a deck that teaches real Commander fundamentals while still letting Celestial Kirin do something exciting and purposeful, The Spiritual Path is a strong place to start.

posture.body_text:
This deck operates under a Control posture within the exploit triangle. It is not trying to race the table and it is not trying to win through redundant pressure. It is trying to suppress opposing plans, derive advantage through superior card quality and interaction over time, and convert only after the table's cleanest lines have been meaningfully constrained.

Control wins by patience. Its cards do not need to be the fastest if they are the most relevant. The posture is built around interaction, timing, and turning better answers into a game state where your opponents are no longer progressing on their own terms.

Against Combo (Assembly) decks, you are advantaged. Their specific pieces and timing windows are exactly what control is trying to break up. In these games, your role is to identify what actually matters and spend interaction where it keeps the table from changing the status quo in their favor.

Against Aggro (Compression) decks, you are being exploited. They want to force action before your slower, higher-quality plan can fully matter. Here, you must survive first, stabilize second, and only then begin to turn that stability into control of the whole game. If you mistake activity for relevance, they will get underneath you.

Your success with this deck comes from knowing what must be stopped, what can be ignored, and when patient play has finally created a position worth converting.

introduction.body_text:
In Magic: the Gathering, White represents structure, order, and the pursuit of stability through rules, balance, and disciplined play.

With Sram, Senior Edificer at the center, To the Drawing Board applies those ideas through an aggro posture built on repeated pressure, initiative, and forcing the table to answer your game before it can comfortably develop its own.

This deck wins by applying pressure over and over again.

Rather than assembling a narrow line where A + B + C must come together, aggro is built more like A, A, A, A: many cards that may look similar in function, overlap in role, and keep asking the same hard question. The strength is not specificity. It is redundancy. Even when individual cards differ, they point in the same direction and let you keep pressing the issue.

Because of this, your goal is not to wait for a perfect hand. Your goal is to identify who has the impetus to act, where your pressure matters most, and how to turn initiative into contraction before slower plans can stabilize. Going for it is broader here. Threat assessment, target sequencing, and recognizing who is actually under pressure matter as much as raw damage.

This deck rewards tempo awareness, initiative, and understanding when the correct play is to keep the table reacting to you instead of giving it time to improve.

This is a pressure-driven deck. It rewards committing threats, recognizing who must act first, and understanding that broad pressure often matters more than a single oversized play. Every game asks you to decide where the beats belong and how to keep opponents from reclaiming the initiative.

If you want a deck that teaches real Commander fundamentals while still letting Sram, Senior Edificer do something exciting and purposeful, To the Drawing Board is a strong place to start.

posture.body_text:
This deck operates under an Aggro posture within the exploit triangle. It is not trying to patiently sculpt a narrow engine or spend the early game suppressing everything at the table. It is trying to create contraction by applying meaningful pressure before slower plans can comfortably stabilize.

Aggro is best understood as A, A, A, A: not because every card is identical, but because many pieces play similar roles and keep asking the same question. Redundancy is the point. Even when the individual cards differ, they overlap in function and let pressure keep flowing.

Against Control (Suppression) decks, you are advantaged. Your job is to force them to answer broad pressure before their higher-quality, slower plan can fully take hold. In these games, initiative matters, and threat assessment helps you decide where pressure changes the table most.

Against Combo (Assembly) decks, you are being exploited. They are trying to use your broader pressure window to buy time for a more specific conversion. Here, you cannot treat every attack the same. You must identify who actually has the impetus to act, decide where the beats belong, and pressure the player most likely to steal the game if left alone.

Your success with this deck comes from recognizing when 'going for it' means committing harder, sequencing threats so they matter, and refusing to give the table easy time it has not earned.

introduction.body_text:
In Magic: the Gathering, White represents structure, order, and the pursuit of stability through rules, balance, and disciplined play.

With Kemba, Kha Regent at the center, Weird Cat Lady applies those ideas through a midrange posture built on flexibility, resilience, and staying live across a wide spread of game states.

This deck wins by staying live across a wide range of game states.

It is not built around a single narrow A + B + C assembly, and it is not just A, A, A, A pressure repeated until someone breaks. Midrange succeeds by combining useful material, flexible interaction, and resilient threats so that your cards remain relevant even as roles shift around the table.

Because of this, your goal is to preserve flexibility, capture parity breaks when they appear, and avoid becoming the clean exploit target in the pod. Going for it with midrange is often about role recognition: knowing when you are the beats, when you are the brakes, and when a stable position should be converted into a faster finish.

This deck rewards adaptability, sequencing, and the ability to shift gears without losing the thread of the game.

why_play.body_text:
You should play this deck if you enjoy flexible games, layered decisions, and always feeling like you have a live draw.

This is an adaptable deck. It asks you to shift roles well, preserve useful material, and recognize when a small parity break should become pressure, value, or control. Every game becomes a question of staying the least exploitable player at the table until your position is strong enough to convert.

These decks are great choices for players learning Commander because staying in one color lets you focus on sequencing, threat assessment, and card interaction without also managing a complicated mana base or color fixing.

If you want a deck that teaches real Commander fundamentals while still letting Kemba, Kha Regent do something exciting and purposeful, Weird Cat Lady is a strong place to start.

posture.body_text:
This deck operates under a Midrange posture within the exploit triangle. It is not defined by the narrow specificity of A + B + C assembly, and it is not built on the repeated redundancy of A, A, A, A pressure. It is trying to stay flexible, preserve useful material, and remain the least clean exploit target at the table.

Midrange sits at the center of the exploit system because its strength is stability. It gives up some ceiling in exchange for fewer hard structural weaknesses, and it wins by adapting better than the pod as roles shift.

That means posture matters differently here. Some games will ask you to be the beats player. Others will ask you to absorb pressure, trade efficiently, and let your card quality or battlefield presence carry the long game. Midrange succeeds when it recognizes which role actually preserves advantage instead of clinging to the same plan every game.

Against more polarized decks, your job is to deny easy exploitation. Against combo, that may mean interacting earlier than you would prefer. Against aggro, that may mean becoming sturdier and forcing them into worse contractions. Against control, that may mean refusing to let the game become about their timing alone.

Your success with this deck comes from recognizing what the table is about, shifting roles cleanly, and converting parity breaks without giving away your flexibility.

introduction.headline_label: GREEN — GO BIG OR GO HOME
introduction.body_text:
In Magic: the Gathering, Green represents growth, inevitability, and the pursuit of strength through natural development. It excels at building overwhelming presence over time by expanding resources and reinforcing its position on the board.

With Titania, Protector of Argoth at the center, Go Big or Go Home applies those ideas through a midrange posture built on flexibility, resilience, and staying live across a wide spread of game states.

This deck wins by staying live across a wide range of game states.

It is not built around a single narrow A + B + C assembly, and it is not just A, A, A, A pressure repeated until someone breaks. Midrange succeeds by combining useful material, flexible interaction, and resilient threats so that your cards remain relevant even as roles shift around the table.

Because of this, your goal is to preserve flexibility, capture parity breaks when they appear, and avoid becoming the clean exploit target in the pod. Going for it with midrange is often about role recognition: knowing when you are the beats, when you are the brakes, and when a stable position should be converted into a faster finish.

This deck rewards adaptability, sequencing, and the ability to shift gears without losing the thread of the game.

This is a pressure-driven deck. It rewards committing threats, recognizing who must act first, and understanding that broad pressure often matters more than a single oversized play. Every game asks you to decide where the beats belong and how to keep opponents from reclaiming the initiative.

This deck asks more from its pilot. Five-color Commander rewards strong planning around color fixing, sequencing, and opening hands, and that added complexity is part of the appeal.

If you want a deck that rewards planning, mana discipline, and high-complexity role recognition, Urrr Dergens offers that experience in a form that still follows Polyhedron's curated pod philosophy.

posture.body_text:
This deck operates under an Aggro posture within the exploit triangle. It is not trying to patiently sculpt a narrow engine or spend the early game suppressing everything at the table. It is trying to create contraction by applying meaningful pressure before slower plans can comfortably stabilize.

Aggro is best understood as A, A, A, A: not because every card is identical, but because many pieces play similar roles and keep asking the same question. Redundancy is the point. Even when the individual cards differ, they overlap in function and let pressure keep flowing.

Against Control (Suppression) decks, you are advantaged. Your job is to force them to answer broad pressure before their higher-quality, slower plan can fully take hold. In these games, initiative matters, and threat assessment helps you decide where pressure changes the table most.

Against Combo (Assembly) decks, you are being exploited. They are trying to use your broader pressure window to buy time for a more specific conversion. Here, you cannot treat every attack the same. You must identify who actually has the impetus to act, decide where the beats belong, and pressure the player most likely to steal the game if left alone.

Your success with this deck comes from recognizing when 'going for it' means committing harder, sequencing threats so they matter, and refusing to give the table easy time it has not earned.

introduction.body_text:
Five-color Magic represents total access: the full spread of tools, philosophies, and strategic options the game can offer. It rewards players who can manage complexity without losing structural discipline. Five-color decks commonly express themselves through broad access, demanding mana systems, flexible interaction suites, and the ability to route toward the line best suited to the table.

With Sisay, Weatherlight Captain at the center, Legendary applies those ideas through a combo posture built on assembly, timing, and the compounding value of getting the right distinct pieces to work together.

This deck wins by assembling pieces.

Rather than relying on a single powerful card, it is built around interactions where A + B + C produces a result greater than any individual part. Combo pieces are specific, different, and additive: one card advances the engine, another enables it, and a third converts it. Once those roles line up, small actions begin to carry much more weight than they appear to on their own.

Because of this, your goal is not simply to deploy threats. Your goal is to identify which role is missing, assemble those pieces efficiently, and protect the system long enough for it to take over the game. Going for it is conditional. The best combo pilots recognize when timing is right, when opponents are constrained, and when the status quo favors committing to conversion instead of waiting.

This deck rewards sequencing, planning, and recognizing when your position has shifted from setup to conversion. When your pieces are in place, even a small spell or routine action can become the start of a winning line.

posture.body_text:
This deck operates under a Combo posture within the exploit triangle. It is not trying to win through early pressure or long-term suppression. It is trying to assemble a functional state and convert it into a win.

Combo is best understood as A + B + C: distinct pieces, different roles, additive functions. Your cards do not all need to do the same thing. They need to complete each other. That is why timing matters so much. Going for it is conditional, and strong combo play is built around recognizing when the table is too constrained to stop clean conversion.

Against Aggro (Compression) decks, you are advantaged once your engine is online. Their plan is to reduce your resources quickly, but a live combo engine can stabilize and then outscale that pressure. In these matchups, your role is to survive long enough to assemble.

Against Control (Suppression) decks, you are being exploited. They are trying to prevent your pieces from functioning together. Here, you must identify when you are the beats player, recognize that waiting forever favors them, and commit to assembly before the status quo hardens against you.

Your success with this deck comes from correctly identifying your role in each game: assemble when you have time, accelerate when you do not, and convert immediately when the window opens.

introduction.body_text:
Five-color Magic represents total access: the full spread of tools, philosophies, and strategic options the game can offer. It rewards players who can manage complexity without losing structural discipline. Five-color decks commonly express themselves through broad access, demanding mana systems, flexible interaction suites, and the ability to route toward the line best suited to the table.

With Reaper King at the center, Poser applies those ideas through a midrange posture built on flexibility, resilience, and staying live across a wide spread of game states.

This deck wins by staying live across a wide range of game states.

It is not built around a single narrow A + B + C assembly, and it is not just A, A, A, A pressure repeated until someone breaks. Midrange succeeds by combining useful material, flexible interaction, and resilient threats so that your cards remain relevant even as roles shift around the table.

Because of this, your goal is to preserve flexibility, capture parity breaks when they appear, and avoid becoming the clean exploit target in the pod. Going for it with midrange is often about role recognition: knowing when you are the beats, when you are the brakes, and when a stable position should be converted into a faster finish.

This deck rewards adaptability, sequencing, and the ability to shift gears without losing the thread of the game.

posture.body_text:
This deck operates under a Midrange posture within the exploit triangle. It is not defined by the narrow specificity of A + B + C assembly, and it is not built on the repeated redundancy of A, A, A, A pressure. It is trying to stay flexible, preserve useful material, and remain the least clean exploit target at the table.

Midrange sits at the center of the exploit system because its strength is stability. It gives up some ceiling in exchange for fewer hard structural weaknesses, and it wins by adapting better than the pod as roles shift.

That means posture matters differently here. Some games will ask you to be the beats player. Others will ask you to absorb pressure, trade efficiently, and let your card quality or battlefield presence carry the long game. Midrange succeeds when it recognizes which role actually preserves advantage instead of clinging to the same plan every game.

Against more polarized decks, your job is to deny easy exploitation. Against combo, that may mean interacting earlier than you would prefer. Against aggro, that may mean becoming sturdier and forcing them into worse contractions. Against control, that may mean refusing to let the game become about their timing alone.

Your success with this deck comes from recognizing what the table is about, shifting roles cleanly, and converting parity breaks without giving away your flexibility.
